essential_question: If you slice an equilateral triangle exactly in half, do the two halves really lock into the 1:√3:2 ratio — and does a real 30-60-90 tool confirm it too?
objective: Construct an equilateral triangle and cut it exactly in half to produce a 30-60-90 triangle (or measure a real 30-60-90 set-square or ramp), measure all three sides, and check that they fit the 1:√3:2 ratio within measurement tolerance.

agenda.title: Brief — From Ratio to Real Cut

content.heading: From the ratio on paper to a triangle you cut yourself
bullets: Yesterday: stated short : long : hypotenuse = 1 : √3 : 2 and used it to find missing sides.
Today: build an equilateral triangle, cut it exactly in half, and measure the 30-60-90 triangle that appears — or measure a real 30-60-90 set-square or ramp instead.
The test: do your own measured sides really land close to 1 : √3 : 2?

content.heading: Gather + plan
content.note: Open the project card. Gather cardstock or paper, a ruler, a compass or string (to construct the equilateral triangle), a protractor or square, and scissors. Decide your equilateral triangle's side length (6 in or 15 cm works well), or pick a real 30-60-90 set-square or ramp to measure instead.

content.heading: Equilateral triangle, split in half
bullets: Construct an equilateral triangle with all three sides equal (use a compass, or measure carefully with a ruler and protractor).
Draw the altitude from one vertex straight down to the midpoint of the opposite side.
Cut along that altitude — you now have two congruent 30-60-90 triangles.
Alternative: if you have access to a real 30-60-90 drafting set-square, or a ramp/roofline built at a 30° or 60° incline, measure its three sides instead of cutting paper.

content.preview: Session 3: derive why the 1:√3:2 ratio has to hold, using the Pythagorean theorem on the equilateral-triangle split, then the checkpoint.

success_criteria.text: I can construct or identify a 30-60-90 triangle by cutting an equilateral triangle exactly in half
I can measure a 30-60-90 triangle's three sides and check they fit 1:√3:2 within measurement tolerance
I can identify which measured side must be the short leg, long leg, and hypotenuse before doing any math

questions.prompt: Construct an equilateral triangle (side length of your choice, e.g. 6 in) and cut it exactly in half along the altitude from one vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side. Measure the three sides of one resulting triangle: the short leg (half the original base), the long leg (the altitude), and the hypotenuse (the original side). Record all three.
questions.answer: For a 6 in equilateral triangle: short leg = 3 in, long leg ≈ 5.2 in, hypotenuse = 6 in.
questions.explain: The altitude bisects both the 60° apex angle (into 30° + 30°) and the base (into two equal halves), and meets the base at a right angle — producing a 30-60-90 triangle whose hypotenuse is the original side and whose short leg is half the original base.

questions.prompt: Divide your measured hypotenuse by your measured short leg. How close is that ratio to exactly 2? Then divide your measured long leg by your short leg — how close is that to √3 ≈ 1.732?
questions.answer: Both ratios should land close to 2 and 1.732 respectively, within normal ruler/cutting tolerance (roughly ±0.1).
questions.explain: Any real cut or measurement carries a little error, but the closer your ratios land to 2 and √3, the more precisely your cut followed the true altitude of the equilateral triangle.

questions.prompt: Before measuring, which of your three sides did you expect to be the hypotenuse, and how did you know — without measuring a single angle?
questions.answer: The original equilateral side, because it's opposite the right angle formed where the altitude meets the base, and it's the longest of the three sides.
questions.explain: The hypotenuse is always the longest side of a right triangle and always sits opposite the 90° angle — that can be spotted before any measuring or math.

questions.prompt: Reflect: which ratio (hypotenuse ÷ short leg, or long leg ÷ short leg) came out closer to its exact value for your build, and what do you think caused any gap — the geometry, or your cutting/measuring?
